Causal and Interpretable Modeling of Influenza Antigenic Distance¶

Separating causal HA escape drivers from linked hitchhikers across three hemagglutination-inhibition datasets, with cross-validated interpretable prediction.

This project was built with Claude Science as part of the Built with Claude: Life Sciences hackathon.

A reproducible research notebook. Run top-to-bottom (Kernel → Restart & Run All) to regenerate every table and figure from the raw data repository shipped alongside this notebook.

Abstract¶

Antibodies neutralize influenza by binding the hemagglutinin (HA) head; the virus escapes by substituting the residues those antibodies recognize. The hemagglutination-inhibition (HI) assay measures the resulting antigenic distance between a virus and a reference strain. A central question for surveillance and vaccine-strain selection is which HA positions causally drive antigenic distance, as opposed to positions that merely correlate with it because they co-evolve with the true drivers along the same phylogeny.

We analyze three HI datasets — VHID H3N2 and the Bedford H3N2 and H1N1 panels — encoded as per-position feature matrices against reference strains. We (1) benchmark how much of the HI signal is multivariable and nonlinear; (2) run target-oriented causal discovery (PC, GES, and where tractable FCI) after collapsing near- deterministic linkage, ranking candidates by bootstrap stability; (3) fit a genuine B-spline Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN) for interpretable nonlinear prediction with per-position response curves — extended to second order to capture and visualize position×position epistasis, which closes the accuracy gap to gradient boosting while remaining interpretable; and (4) reconcile the methods by cross-method convergence.

The strongest claims are HA positions flagged by all four independent method families (causal high-confidence, KAN, gradient boosting, univariate association): positions 156 and 189 in VHID H3N2; 143, 168, 199 in Bedford H3N2; and 151 in Bedford H1N1 — each a singleton linkage block sitting in or adjacent to classical head antigenic sites. Backdoor-adjusted effect sizes are systematically smaller than marginal associations, quantifying the phylogenetic confounding the causal framing is designed to remove. We validate the discovered structure with four complementary tests — d-separation goodness-of-fit, bootstrap stability, split-sample direct effects, and leave-one-era-out out-of-distribution transport — the last showing the discovered parent sets are largely recovered on held-out collection eras (Jaccard = 1.0 on most folds), evidence they are not merely era-specific hitchhikers, while their effect magnitudes drift across eras. All causal results are reported as bootstrap-ranked equivalence-class candidates under a Gaussian working model, never as a single settled graph.

1. Introduction¶

Influenza viruses evolve without pause. Under the selective pressure of population immunity, variants that carry new substitutions in their surface glycoproteins gain a transmission advantage, and the circulating population ratchets steadily away from the strains our immune systems have learned to recognize — the process called antigenic drift. Drift is what erodes vaccine protection: seasonal vaccines are formulated months in advance against the strains expected to circulate, and in years when the chosen strain no longer matches the viruses that actually spread, effectiveness falls. Crucially, this mismatch is not an all-or-nothing quantity but a graded one — the antigenic distance between the vaccine strain and a circulating strain — so measuring that distance accurately is a prerequisite for interpreting vaccine effectiveness and, ultimately, for designing vaccines that protect broadly against strains not yet seen.

Measuring antigenic distance, however, is itself difficult. The reference approach, antigenic cartography, embeds large tables of serological measurements into a low-dimensional map whose geometry recovers the distance between strains; it has been indispensable for understanding influenza evolution but demands serum panels and cross-titrations that are slow and costly to generate. The measurement underlying nearly all of these maps is the hemagglutination-inhibition (HI) titer — the dilution of a reference antiserum that still blocks a virus from agglutinating red blood cells — where a lower cross-titer signals greater antigenic distance, that is, escape. Because serology is expensive and imperfectly reproducible between laboratories, a long line of work has asked whether antigenic distance might instead be read directly from the hemagglutinin (HA) sequence. If it could, surveillance could flag an escape variant the moment its sequence appeared, before any assay is run.

This is precisely where prediction and explanation part ways. A sequence-based model can forecast HI titer accurately while revealing nothing about why a strain has escaped, because HA positions are strongly linked: strains share a phylogeny, so a position that merely rides along with a true escape driver looks just as predictive as the driver itself. Standard association tools — LASSO, gradient boosting — answer "which positions predict titer", not "which positions cause the titer change"; yet it is the causal question that matters both for mechanism and for anticipating escape variants that have never been observed. This study asks which individual HA positions causally drive antigenic distance, and it defends each answer by requiring agreement across four independent method families rather than trusting any single model — recovering, from HI titers alone and with no structural prior beyond the direction of causation, positions that sit squarely on the classical head epitopes.

Related works. Antigenic cartography (Smith et al., 2004) revealed the punctuated cluster structure of H3N2 drift by embedding HI tables into a low-dimensional antigenic map, and Bedford et al. (2014) unified antigenic and genetic evolution within a joint phylogenetic model. More recent sequence-based predictors, such as DPCIPI (Du et al., 2023), forecast cross-immunity between drifted strains from sequence alone with strong accuracy. These approaches are predictive or descriptive: they map, model, or forecast antigenic distance, but none performs explicit causal structure learning over individual HA positions with quantified stability — the step that distinguishes a genuine escape driver from a co-evolving position that merely accompanies it along the shared phylogeny.

Our contribution. We combine three ingredients not usually applied together to HI data. First, linkage collapse with target-oriented causal discovery: we merge near-deterministic co-evolving positions into representative loci, then learn the direct-cause neighborhood of the HI target with PC, GES, and — where tractable — FCI, ranking every candidate by 200× bootstrap stability. Second, a genuine B-spline Kolmogorov–Arnold Network (KAN): an interpretable nonlinear predictor whose learned per-position response curves are directly inspectable, extended to second order to capture and visualize position×position epistasis. Third, cross-method convergence: positions independently flagged by causal discovery, the KAN, gradient boosting, and univariate association are the defensible causal-driver claims, and their disagreements are themselves informative. We further estimate backdoor-adjusted per-position effect sizes, exploiting the fact that the HI target is a causal sink so its other parents form a valid adjustment set.

The remainder of the notebook is organized as an executable paper. Section 2 (Methods) introduces the three HI datasets and how their feature matrices are prepared from raw data. Section 3 (Results) then carries the analysis in full: each subsection states how one step is performed, presents its output, and interprets it — the predictive benchmark and its cross-validated comparison, target-oriented causal discovery and its dependency-structure test, the B-spline KAN and its second-order epistasis extension, cross-method convergence, backdoor-adjusted effect sizes, and four DAG-validation tests — moving from how much of the HI signal is learnable and how nonlinear it is, through the discovered causal drivers and their dependency structure, to per-position effect sizes and the validation of the discovered graph. Section 4 (Conclusion) interprets the convergent drivers biologically, states where residue-level attribution is limited and what assumptions the causal claims rest on, and looks ahead. Section 5 lists references.

2. Methods¶

This section describes the data. We introduce the three HI panels and how their per-position feature matrices are prepared from raw sequence data; the analytical procedures that consume these matrices are described step-by-step in the Results section, where each is presented alongside the output it produces. Configuration (random seed, linkage threshold, bootstrap count, tier cutoffs) is centralized in src/analysis.py, and two expensive steps — the causal bootstrap (RECOMPUTE_CAUSAL, the H3N2 60-locus graph takes ~30 min) and the repeated k-fold cross-validation (RECOMPUTE_CV, ~40 min) — are gated behind environment flags, loading shipped results/ artifacts by default so a full top-to-bottom run is fast and deterministic.

2.1 Datasets¶

The study uses three virus × reference-strain HI panels, each shipped in the data repository as per-position HA1 feature matrices with two encodings. The binary mismatch encoding sets a position to 1 when the virus and reference residues differ at that HA1 alignment position and 0 otherwise, and is used for causal discovery. The Grantham encoding gives the Grantham (1974) physicochemical distance between the two residues (gap-aware), and is used for predictive modeling and the KAN response curves. The final column of every matrix is the raw HI_titer; we model log2(HI_titer). The three study datasets map to the repository as VHID H3N2 → VHID/, Bedford H3N2 → Bedford/H3/, and Bedford H1N1 → Bedford/H1/ (the repo also ships B/Yamagata and B/Victoria, outside this study's scope). We begin by importing the analysis module and fixing configuration.

In [1]:
import os, sys, json
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
%matplotlib inline

sys.path.insert(0, "src")
import analysis as A

# quiet, reproducible
import warnings; warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
np.random.seed(A.SEED)
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 110, "font.size": 9, "axes.spines.top": False,
                     "axes.spines.right": False})
COLORS = {"LASSO": "#7fb3d5", "Ridge": "#5499c7", "XGBoost": "#e67e22",
          "KAN": "#c0392b", "causal": "#c0392b"}

print("Datasets in this study:", A.DATASETS)
print("Data directory:", os.path.relpath(A.DATA_DIR, A.REPO_ROOT))
print("Recompute causal from scratch:", A.RECOMPUTE_CAUSAL)

Datasets in this study: ['vhid_HA1', 'H3N2', 'H1N1']
Data directory: influenza-hi-antigenic-distance
Recompute causal from scratch: True

2.1.1 Reproducing the feature matrices from raw data¶

So that the study starts from raw data rather than shipped derivatives, we regenerate the feature matrices from the cleaned virus × reference pair tables using the repository's own build scripts (scripts/build_vhid_matrices.py, scripts/build_bedford_matrices.py), which depend only on numpy and pandas. Running them here makes the matrices provably the shipped ones (verified by SHA-256).

In [2]:
# Regenerate the matrices from the cleaned pair tables (idempotent; ~seconds).
A.rebuild_matrices_from_raw()
data = A.load_all()

summary = pd.DataFrame([
    dict(dataset=ds, source=A.DATASET_LABEL[ds], n_pairs=data[ds]["n"],
         HA1_positions=data[ds]["n_pos"],
         variant_positions=len(A.variant_columns(data[ds]["Xb"])),
         log2_titer_mean=round(float(data[ds]["y"].mean()), 3),
         log2_titer_std=round(float(data[ds]["y"].std()), 3))
    for ds in A.DATASETS])
summary

  grantham -> VHID/vhid_HA1_grantham_HImatrix.csv  shape=(2751, 330)  residual_NaN=0
[Vic]   binary shape=(517, 347)  grantham shape=(517, 347)  residual_NaN=0
Out[2]: 
    dataset  ... log2_titer_std
0  vhid_HA1  ...          2.861
1      H3N2  ...          2.286
2      H1N1  ...          1.746

[3 rows x 7 columns]
Out[2]:
dataset source n_pairs HA1_positions variant_positions log2_titer_mean log2_titer_std
0 vhid_HA1 VHID H3N2 (Du et al. 2023) 2751 329 102 7.256 2.861
1 H3N2 Bedford H3N2 (Bedford et al. 2014) 7808 329 312 7.770 2.286
2 H1N1 Bedford H1N1 (Bedford et al. 2014) 913 327 70 7.644 1.746

Each dataset is internally row-aligned across its binary matrix, Grantham matrix, and cleaned pair table. Feature spaces are comparable within a lineage but not across lineages (each lineage was HA1-trimmed against its own reference), so we analyze the three datasets independently and compare only which HA positions emerge.

2.1.2 The target distribution¶

Before any modeling we look at what is being predicted. The panel below shows the distribution of log2 HI titer in each dataset; its spread sets the scale against which every R² reported in the Results should be read.

In [3]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    ax.hist(data[ds]["y"], bins=30, color="#5499c7", edgecolor="white")
    ax.set_title(f"{ds}  (n={data[ds]['n']})", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xlabel("log2 HI titer"); ax.set_ylabel("pairs" if ds == A.DATASETS[0] else "")
fig.suptitle("Distribution of the modeling target (log2 HI titer) per dataset", y=1.03, fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "target_distribution.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

3. Results¶

We now walk through each analytical step and report what it produced. Because the Methods section covers only the data, each subsection below opens by stating how the step is done, then presents its output and interprets it — moving from prediction to cause: first how much of the HI signal is learnable and how nonlinear it is, which fixes what any mechanistic account must explain; then the positions that plausibly drive the signal and their dependency structure; then the interpretable predictor and the epistasis it exposes; and finally the convergent driver claims, their confounding-adjusted effect sizes, and the validation of the discovered graph. No number or figure is presented without interpreting how it supports the point.

3.1 Predictive benchmark¶

We first establish how much of the HI signal is learnable and whether it is multivariable and nonlinear. For each dataset we compare, on a held-out 20% test split, the best single position (max univariate R²), LASSO, Ridge, and XGBoost.

The benchmark below reports, for each dataset, the held-out test R² of the best single position, LASSO, Ridge, and XGBoost.

In [4]:
bench = {ds: A.benchmark(data, ds) for ds in A.DATASETS}
bench_tbl = pd.DataFrame([dict(dataset=ds, **bench[ds]) for ds in A.DATASETS])
bench_tbl = bench_tbl[["dataset", "n", "n_features", "univ_sig",
                       "univ_best_singleR2", "LASSO_testR2", "Ridge_testR2", "XGB_testR2"]]
bench_tbl.round(3)

Out[4]: 
    dataset     n  n_features  ...  LASSO_testR2  Ridge_testR2  XGB_testR2
0  vhid_HA1  2751         102  ...         0.751         0.754       0.862
1      H3N2  7808         312  ...         0.509         0.522       0.618
2      H1N1   913          70  ...         0.444         0.419       0.562

[3 rows x 8 columns]
Out[4]:
dataset n n_features univ_sig univ_best_singleR2 LASSO_testR2 Ridge_testR2 XGB_testR2
0 vhid_HA1 2751 102 78 0.443 0.751 0.754 0.862
1 H3N2 7808 312 185 0.205 0.509 0.522 0.618
2 H1N1 913 70 56 0.167 0.444 0.419 0.562

In every dataset the ordering is XGBoost ≳ LASSO ≈ Ridge ≫ best single position. The large gap from best-single-position to the multivariable models shows the signal is distributed across many positions; the XGBoost margin over the linear models indicates nonlinear / interaction structure — the motivation for the KAN (Section 3.5). We add the KAN test R² to this comparison in Section 3.5.

3.2 Cross-validated R² with confidence intervals¶

A single split gives a point estimate but no uncertainty, so we repeat k-fold cross-validation to attach 95% confidence intervals to each method's R². LASSO, Ridge, and XGBoost use 5×4 repeated k-fold; the KAN uses 3-fold (GPU cost). The repeated k-fold fold scores are loaded from results/cv_r2_folds.json by default (recompute with RECOMPUTE_CV=1, ~40 min); the cv_linear_tree function below is the exact code that produced them, and the KAN folds are always loaded from results/ (GPU-trained in the study).

The cross-validated intervals below test whether the single-split ordering holds under resampling.

In [5]:
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.model_selection import RepeatedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV, Ridge
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
import xgboost as xgb

def cv_linear_tree(ds, n_splits=5, n_repeats=4, seed=A.SEED):
    d = data[ds]; cols = A.variant_columns(d["Xb"])
    X, y = d["Xg"][:, cols], d["y"]
    rkf = RepeatedKFold(n_splits=n_splits, n_repeats=n_repeats, random_state=seed)
    out = {"LASSO": [], "Ridge": [], "XGBoost": []}
    for tr, te in rkf.split(X):
        sc = StandardScaler().fit(X[tr]); Xtr, Xte = sc.transform(X[tr]), sc.transform(X[te])
        out["LASSO"].append(r2_score(y[te], LassoCV(alphas=np.logspace(-3,0,12), cv=3,
                             max_iter=20000).fit(Xtr, y[tr]).predict(Xte)))
        out["Ridge"].append(r2_score(y[te], Ridge(alpha=10.0).fit(Xtr, y[tr]).predict(Xte)))
        dtr, dte = xgb.DMatrix(X[tr], label=y[tr]), xgb.DMatrix(X[te], label=y[te])
        bst = xgb.train({"max_depth":4,"eta":0.1,"subsample":0.8,"colsample_bytree":0.8,
                         "objective":"reg:squarederror"}, dtr, num_boost_round=300,
                        evals=[(dte,"te")], early_stopping_rounds=20, verbose_eval=False)
        out["XGBoost"].append(r2_score(y[te], bst.predict(dte, iteration_range=(0, bst.best_iteration+1))))
    return out

# The 5x4 repeated k-fold CV is heavy (~40 min, mostly 20 XGBoost fits on the
# 7808x312 H3N2 matrix). Load the study fold scores by default; RECOMPUTE_CV=1 reruns.
saved_folds = A.load_result("cv_r2_folds.json")
if A.RECOMPUTE_CV:
    cv_folds = {ds: cv_linear_tree(ds) for ds in A.DATASETS}
else:
    cv_folds = {ds: {m: saved_folds[ds][m] for m in ["LASSO", "Ridge", "XGBoost"]}
                for ds in A.DATASETS}
# KAN CV folds always loaded from the study (GPU-trained)
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    cv_folds[ds]["KAN"] = saved_folds[ds]["KAN"]
print("CV folds:", "recomputed" if A.RECOMPUTE_CV else "loaded from results/cv_r2_folds.json")

def ci95(v):
    v = np.array(v, float); m = v.mean()
    if len(v) < 2: return m, m, m
    se = v.std(ddof=1)/np.sqrt(len(v)); lo, hi = stats.t.interval(0.95, len(v)-1, loc=m, scale=se)
    return m, lo, hi

cv_rows = []
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    for meth in ["LASSO","Ridge","XGBoost","KAN"]:
        m, lo, hi = ci95(cv_folds[ds][meth])
        cv_rows.append(dict(dataset=ds, method=meth, folds=len(cv_folds[ds][meth]),
                            mean_R2=round(m,4), ci_lo=round(lo,4), ci_hi=round(hi,4)))
cv_tbl = pd.DataFrame(cv_rows)
cv_tbl

CV folds: recomputed
Out[5]: 
     dataset   method  folds  mean_R2   ci_lo   ci_hi
0   vhid_HA1    LASSO     20   0.7386  0.7338  0.7434
1   vhid_HA1    Ridge     20   0.7426  0.7371  0.7482
2   vhid_HA1  XGBoost     20   0.8528  0.8481  0.8575
3   vhid_HA1      KAN      3   0.8230  0.7758  0.8701
4       H3N2    LASSO     20   0.5023  0.4953  0.5093
5       H3N2    Ridge     20   0.5185  0.5121  0.5250
6       H3N2  XGBoost     20   0.6232  0.6158  0.6306
7       H3N2      KAN      3   0.5789  0.5318  0.6259
8       H1N1    LASSO     20   0.5316  0.5080  0.5552
9       H1N1    Ridge     20   0.5307  0.5059  0.5555
10      H1N1  XGBoost     20   0.6208  0.6056  0.6361
11      H1N1      KAN      3   0.5918  0.5077  0.6759
Out[5]:
dataset method folds mean_R2 ci_lo ci_hi
0 vhid_HA1 LASSO 20 0.7386 0.7338 0.7434
1 vhid_HA1 Ridge 20 0.7426 0.7371 0.7482
2 vhid_HA1 XGBoost 20 0.8528 0.8481 0.8575
3 vhid_HA1 KAN 3 0.8230 0.7758 0.8701
4 H3N2 LASSO 20 0.5023 0.4953 0.5093
5 H3N2 Ridge 20 0.5185 0.5121 0.5250
6 H3N2 XGBoost 20 0.6232 0.6158 0.6306
7 H3N2 KAN 3 0.5789 0.5318 0.6259
8 H1N1 LASSO 20 0.5316 0.5080 0.5552
9 H1N1 Ridge 20 0.5307 0.5059 0.5555
10 H1N1 XGBoost 20 0.6208 0.6056 0.6361
11 H1N1 KAN 3 0.5918 0.5077 0.6759
In [6]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))
order = ["LASSO","Ridge","XGBoost","KAN"]
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    sub = cv_tbl[cv_tbl.dataset == ds]; yv = np.arange(len(order))[::-1]
    for yi, meth in zip(yv, order):
        r = sub[sub.method == meth].iloc[0]
        ax.errorbar(r["mean_R2"], yi, xerr=[[r["mean_R2"]-r["ci_lo"]],[r["ci_hi"]-r["mean_R2"]]],
                    fmt="o", color=COLORS[meth], capsize=3, ms=7, mec="white")
        ax.text(r["mean_R2"], yi+0.22, f"{r['mean_R2']:.3f}", ha="center", fontsize=6, color=COLORS[meth])
    ax.set_yticks(yv); ax.set_yticklabels(order); ax.set_title(ds, fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xlabel("Cross-validated R²"); ax.grid(axis="x", ls=":", lw=0.5, alpha=0.6); ax.margins(y=0.18)
fig.suptitle("Cross-validated R² with 95% CIs (LASSO/Ridge/XGBoost: 5×4 repeated k-fold; KAN: 3-fold)", y=1.02, fontsize=9)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "cv_r2.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

XGBoost has the highest cross-validated R² in VHID and H3N2; on H1N1 the KAN's interval overlaps XGBoost's. The intervals are tight for the two larger datasets and wider for H1N1 (n=913), as expected. These CIs confirm the single-split benchmark ordering is not an artifact of one partition.

3.3 Causal discovery¶

We treat HI titer as a causal sink: HA sequence differences cause the titer, not vice-versa. Encoding this as background knowledge orients every feature→target edge into the target, so the target's parents are its direct-cause candidates.

The pipeline is: power filter (drop invariant / rare positions) → linkage collapse (merge positions with |φ|≥0.8 into one representative locus per co-evolving block) → PC (constraint-based) and GES (score-based) discovery → 200× bootstrap for selection-stability tiers. Linkage collapse is the load-bearing step: near-deterministic co-evolution violates faithfulness and makes search intractable, and collapsing restores both. Every causal claim then attaches to a block, so we always report block size. FCI (latent-aware) is run only for VHID, because causal-learn's FCI possible-d-sep step does not terminate in practical time on the denser H3N2/H1N1 collapsed graphs (>30 min at ≥24 nodes); for those we report PC + GES + bootstrap, and very wide graphs are screened to the top loci by target association first.

Running this pipeline yields, per dataset, the collapsed loci, the PC/GES parent sets, and the bootstrap-stability tiers below.

In [7]:
import causal_helpers as ch

collapsed = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    Adf, blocks = A.collapse_to_loci(data, ds)
    # residual near-deterministic locus pairs should be ~0 after collapse
    Xr = Adf.drop(columns="HI_titer").values
    resid = int((np.abs(np.corrcoef(Xr.T))[np.triu_indices(Xr.shape[1], 1)] >= 0.9).sum())
    collapsed[ds] = dict(A=Adf, blocks=blocks, n_loci=Adf.shape[1]-1,
                         n_multiblocks=sum(1 for v in blocks.values() if len(v) > 1),
                         residual_strong_pairs=resid)

collapse_tbl = pd.DataFrame([
    dict(dataset=ds, variant_positions=len(A.variant_columns(data[ds]["Xb"])),
         loci_after_collapse=collapsed[ds]["n_loci"],
         multi_position_blocks=collapsed[ds]["n_multiblocks"],
         residual_strong_pairs=collapsed[ds]["residual_strong_pairs"])
    for ds in A.DATASETS])
collapse_tbl

Out[7]: 
    dataset  variant_positions  ...  multi_position_blocks  residual_strong_pairs
0  vhid_HA1                102  ...                     10                      0
1      H3N2                312  ...                      9                      0
2      H1N1                 70  ...                     11                      0

[3 rows x 5 columns]
Out[7]:
dataset variant_positions loci_after_collapse multi_position_blocks residual_strong_pairs
0 vhid_HA1 102 71 10 0
1 H3N2 312 123 9 0
2 H1N1 70 34 11 0

Residual strong locus pairs (|φ|≥0.9) drop to 0 in all three datasets, confirming that linkage collapse succeeded and restored the conditions discovery needs. PC and GES then run on the collapsed loci, and the bootstrap tiers below rank each candidate.

In [8]:
# PC + GES live (fast). FCI only for vhid. Bootstrap tiers: reuse results/ by default.
causal_saved = A.load_result("causal_results.json")

def run_pc_ges(ds, screen_over=75, screen_k=60):
    Adf = collapsed[ds]["A"]; n_loci = Adf.shape[1]-1
    sk = lambda s: int(s[3:])
    if n_loci > screen_over:
        feats = ch.screen_top_features(Adf, "HI_titer", screen_k)
        A_use = Adf[sorted(feats, key=sk) + ["HI_titer"]]; screened = True
    else:
        A_use = Adf; screened = False
    pc = ch.discover_target_parents(A_use, "HI_titer", method="pc", alpha=0.01)
    ges = ch.ges_target_neighborhood(A_use, "HI_titer", screen_k=20, must_include=pc["parents"])
    ges_par = [f for f, t in ges["neighborhood"].items() if t == "->"]
    return sorted(pc["parents"], key=sk), sorted(ges_par, key=sk), screened, A_use

pc_ges = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    pc_par, ges_par, screened, _ = run_pc_ges(ds)
    pc_ges[ds] = dict(pc=pc_par, ges=ges_par, screened=screened)
    print(f"{ds}: PC={pc_par}")
    print(f"{'':>{len(ds)}}  GES={ges_par}  (screened={screened})")

vhid_HA1: PC=['pos144', 'pos156', 'pos158', 'pos189', 'pos289']
          GES=['pos50', 'pos133', 'pos144', 'pos145', 'pos262', 'pos276']  (screened=False)
H3N2: PC=['pos11', 'pos88', 'pos143', 'pos167', 'pos168', 'pos199', 'pos200', 'pos203', 'pos288']
      GES=['pos93', 'pos143', 'pos167', 'pos168', 'pos182', 'pos207', 'pos286']  (screened=True)
H1N1: PC=['pos53', 'pos135', 'pos151', 'pos199', 'pos232']
      GES=['pos4', 'pos53', 'pos131', 'pos135', 'pos151', 'pos163', 'pos167', 'pos199', 'pos232', 'pos283']  (screened=False)
In [9]:
# Bootstrap stability tiers. Recompute (~30 min on H3N2) or reuse the study results.
if A.RECOMPUTE_CAUSAL:
    boot_freq = {}
    for ds in A.DATASETS:
        _, _, _, A_use = run_pc_ges(ds)
        bs = ch.bootstrap_target_parents(A_use, "HI_titer", B=A.BOOTSTRAP_B,
                                         screen_k=50, must_include=pc_ges[ds]["pc"], n_jobs=8)
        boot_freq[ds] = {int(k[3:]): round(v, 4) for k, v in bs["freq"].items()}
else:
    boot_freq = {ds: {int(k[3:]): v for k, v in causal_saved[ds]["bootstrap_freq"].items()}
                 for ds in A.DATASETS}

blocks_all = {ds: collapsed[ds]["blocks"] for ds in A.DATASETS}

def causal_table(ds):
    hi, md_ = A.tiered_parents(boot_freq[ds])
    pc = set(pc_ges[ds]["pc"]); ges = set(pc_ges[ds]["ges"])
    fci = set(causal_saved[ds]["fci"] or []) if causal_saved[ds].get("fci") else None
    fci_num = set(int(p[3:]) for p in (causal_saved[ds]["fci"] or [])) if fci is not None else None
    rows = []
    for p, v in sorted({**hi, **md_}.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1]):
        pn = f"pos{p}"
        rows.append(dict(position=p, bootstrap_freq=round(v,3),
            tier="high" if v >= A.HIGH_CONF else "moderate",
            PC=pn in pc, GES=pn in ges,
            FCI=(p in fci_num) if fci_num is not None else "n/a",
            block_size=len(blocks_all[ds].get(p, [p]))))
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)

causal_tables = {ds: causal_table(ds) for ds in A.DATASETS}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    print(f"=== {ds} (FCI {'run' if causal_saved[ds].get('fci') else 'omitted — see text'}) ===")
    display(causal_tables[ds])

=== vhid_HA1 (FCI run) ===
=== H3N2 (FCI omitted — see text) ===
=== H1N1 (FCI omitted — see text) ===
position bootstrap_freq tier PC GES FCI block_size
0 156 1.000 high True False True 1
1 189 1.000 high True False True 1
2 289 0.955 high True False True 1
3 158 0.950 high True False True 1
4 144 0.505 moderate True True False 1
position bootstrap_freq tier PC GES FCI block_size
0 11 1.000 high True False n/a 9
1 143 1.000 high True True n/a 1
2 167 1.000 high True True n/a 1
3 168 1.000 high True True n/a 1
4 199 1.000 high True False n/a 1
5 203 0.995 high True False n/a 1
6 288 0.815 moderate True False n/a 1
7 200 0.650 moderate True False n/a 1
position bootstrap_freq tier PC GES FCI block_size
0 151 1.000 high True True n/a 1
1 135 0.825 moderate True True n/a 1
2 53 0.795 moderate True True n/a 5
3 199 0.520 moderate True True n/a 1

The bootstrap frequency tiers each candidate into high (≥0.9), moderate (0.5–0.9), or unstable. High-confidence parents are: VHID {156, 189, 289, 158}; H3N2 {11, 143, 167, 168, 199, 203}; H1N1 {151}. Positions with block_size > 1 (e.g. H3N2 pos11 = 9-position block, H1N1 pos53 = 5) carry claims for the whole block, not the single position. GES independently confirms the PC parents 143/167/168 in H3N2 and 151/135/53 in H1N1.

3.4 The discovered dependency structure¶

We visualize each dataset's learned structure as a titer-sink graph: every retained direct-cause candidate is a colored arrow into the HI-titer node, with width and opacity proportional to its bootstrap stability and color by tier. But is this a strict star — positions that each affect titer while being mutually independent? We test that directly rather than assume it, running a partial-correlation skeleton over each parent set plus titer, which asks two questions:

  1. Are any parents pure intermediates? A position that reaches titer only through another (posₐ → pos_P → titer, no direct edge) would become conditionally independent of titer once the other parents are conditioned on, and drop out. We test each parent against titer given all other parents.
  2. Are the parents mutually independent? We test every parent–parent pair for adjacency given all the other parents (excluding titer — since titer is a common child of the parents, conditioning on it would induce spurious dependence by the collider / explaining-away effect). Any surviving edge means the star is incomplete.

The grey arcs overlaid on the graph are the significant parent–parent adjacencies from test 2. They are drawn without arrowheads: a partial correlation cannot distinguish posₐ → pos_b from shared-ancestry confounding (posₐ ← L → pos_b), so their direction is not identified.

The titer-sink graphs below show each dataset's retained direct-cause candidates and the parent–parent adjacencies from the skeleton test.

In [10]:
skel = {ds: A.parent_skeleton(ds, data, causal_saved) for ds in A.DATASETS}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4.6))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    A.draw_titer_dag(ax, ds, boot_freq[ds], blocks_all[ds], among_edges=skel[ds]["among"])
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
leg = [Line2D([0],[0],marker="o",color="w",mfc="#c0392b",ms=9,label="High conf (boot≥0.9)"),
       Line2D([0],[0],marker="o",color="w",mfc="#e6924b",ms=9,label="Moderate (0.5–0.9)"),
       Line2D([0],[0],color="#7f8c8d",lw=2,alpha=0.6,label="parent–parent adjacency (undirected)")]
fig.legend(handles=leg, ncol=3, fontsize=7.5, frameon=False, loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5,1.04))
fig.suptitle("Discovered titer-sink structure. Colored arrow = direct-cause candidate "
             "(width/opacity ∝ bootstrap freq, shown at left; * = linkage block); "
             "grey arc = significant parent–parent adjacency (undirected).",
             fontsize=7.3, y=-0.02)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "dag.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

# report the two structural tests
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    s = skel[ds]; nd = len(s["parents"]); ne = len(s["among"])
    n_direct = sum(1 for r, p in s["direct"].values() if p < 0.01)
    print(f"{ds}: {n_direct}/{nd} parents remain directly titer-adjacent given the others "
          f"(no pure mediators); {ne} of {nd*(nd-1)//2} parent-parent pairs are adjacent "
          f"=> {'NOT a star (parents interdependent)' if ne else 'star (parents independent)'}")

vhid_HA1: 5/5 parents remain directly titer-adjacent given the others (no pure mediators); 5 of 10 parent-parent pairs are adjacent => NOT a star (parents interdependent)
H3N2: 8/8 parents remain directly titer-adjacent given the others (no pure mediators); 21 of 28 parent-parent pairs are adjacent => NOT a star (parents interdependent)
H1N1: 4/4 parents remain directly titer-adjacent given the others (no pure mediators); 4 of 6 parent-parent pairs are adjacent => NOT a star (parents interdependent)

The result is consistent across all three datasets: every parent stays directly associated with titer conditional on the others — so the parent set contains no pure intermediates, and each carries its own direct effect (matching the nonzero backdoor estimates in §3.8). But the parents are densely interdependent — most parent–parent pairs are adjacent — so the structure is not a strict star. This is precisely why the goodness-of-fit test in §3.9 rejects the bare star, and the linear parent–parent adjacencies here are the first-order shadow of the same interdependence the second-order KAN captures nonlinearly as the epistasis surfaces in §3.6 (the 144×189 pair, for instance, is both a significant parent–parent adjacency here and a top-ranked KAN interaction surface; note the two views need not coincide — a linear partial-correlation edge and a nonlinear interaction surface measure related but distinct things, so most pairs appear in only one). What the observational data cannot resolve is the direction of these inter-parent links or whether any parent is a partial mediator (a direct effect plus an indirect one through a neighbor); separating those would require edge orientation or intervention.

3.4.1 Expanding causal survivors to their 12 property axes¶

Causal discovery (§3.3–3.4) ran deliberately at one scalar per position — the Grantham distance — because feeding twelve correlated property axes per site into PC/FCI would stack within-position collinearity on top of the cross-position linkage the collapse step already handles, worsening the faithfulness violations the method is built to avoid. That keeps discovery tractable and faithful, but it leaves each surviving position described by a single lumped distance.

The bridge is the hybrid's second half. We take only the positions that survived causal discovery (bootstrap frequency ≥ 0.9, each collapsed locus expanded through its linkage block to member positions) and re-express each one in its full 12-dimensional property encoding from the Amino Acid Transition Vector Toolkit (src/aa_transition_vector_toolkit.py, scales in src/aa_properties.json): Grantham composition/polarity/volume, Kyte–Doolittle hydropathy, charge, Chou–Fasman helix/sheet/turn propensity, aromaticity, flexibility, and side-chain H-bond donor/acceptor counts. Each feature is the change property(virus) − property(reference) at that site, named pos{k}__{property} (e.g. pos156__charge).

This is the right division of labour: discovery stays low-dimensional and faithful, while interpretation becomes high-resolution — the KAN in §3.5 can now attribute a survivor's effect to a specific property at a specific site ("charge reversal at position 156") rather than a single distance. Property axes that never vary at a given survivor site (e.g. aromaticity where no aromatic substitution occurs) carry no signal and are dropped.

In [11]:
# Step A -> B bridge: expand each dataset's high-confidence causal survivors
# (bootstrap freq >= 0.9, linkage-block-expanded) to their 12 property axes.
# Build the position x property matrices once here; reused by 3.4.2, 3.5, 3.6.2.
prop_mats = A.load_property_matrices()
kan_bridge = {ds: A.build_kan_hybrid_input(ds, causal=causal_saved, prop=prop_mats[ds])
              for ds in A.DATASETS}

bridge_tbl = pd.DataFrame([{
    "dataset": ds,
    "causal survivors": len(kan_bridge[ds]["survivors"]),
    "survivor positions": ", ".join(map(str, kan_bridge[ds]["survivors"])),
    "property features (survivors x 12)": kan_bridge[ds]["X_scaled"].shape[1],
    "constant axes dropped": len(kan_bridge[ds]["dropped_constant"]),
} for ds in A.DATASETS])
display(bridge_tbl)
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    b = kan_bridge[ds]
    print(f"{ds}: {len(b['survivors'])} survivors x 12 -> {b['X_scaled'].shape[1]} property features "
          f"(dropped {len(b['dropped_constant'])} zero-variance axes)")

vhid_HA1: 4 survivors x 12 -> 44 property features (dropped 4 zero-variance axes)
H3N2: 14 survivors x 12 -> 142 property features (dropped 26 zero-variance axes)
H1N1: 1 survivors x 12 -> 11 property features (dropped 1 zero-variance axes)
dataset causal survivors survivor positions property features (survivors x 12) constant axes dropped
0 vhid_HA1 4 156, 158, 189, 289 44 4
1 H3N2 14 11, 64, 143, 153, 156, 167, 168, 173, 199, 203... 142 26
2 H1N1 1 151 11 1

3.4.2 Causal discovery at property resolution (the 12D encoding)¶

The DAG in §3.4 discovers which positions are direct causes of titer, using a single scalar (Grantham distance) per position. A natural next question — and one raised in review — is which physicochemical axis at each causal position carries the effect. Here we repeat the same target-oriented causal discovery, but replace each survivor position's scalar Grantham distance with its 12-dimensional property-change vector from the Step A→B bridge (§3.4.1). The PC parents of HI_titer_log2 are then specific pos{k}__{property} axes, not whole positions.

One subtlety must be handled first. The 12 property axes at a single position are near-collinear — composition, polarity and volume are all deterministic functions of the same amino-acid substitution — so the survivors×12 matrix is badly rank-deficient and the Fisher-z conditional-independence test hits a singular correlation matrix. We therefore run a rank-revealing (column-pivoted) QR first to keep only a well-conditioned, numerically independent subset of property axes before discovery.

In [12]:
# 12D-property causal discovery: PC + terminal background knowledge on the causal
# survivors' property axes (rank-revealing QR decorrelation first). Bootstrap the
# edge selection to tier each property axis by stability.
causal12d = {ds: A.causal_discovery_12d(ds, causal=causal_saved, prop=prop_mats[ds])
             for ds in A.DATASETS}
boot12d = {ds: A.bootstrap_causal_12d(ds, causal=causal_saved, prop=prop_mats[ds],
                                      B=A.BOOTSTRAP_B) for ds in A.DATASETS}

# Property-resolution DAG: survivor positions -> retained property axes -> HI titer,
# colouring each axis by its bootstrap stability.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13.5, 4.8))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    freq = boot12d[ds]["freq"]
    axesorted = sorted(freq.items(), key=lambda t: -t[1])[:8]
    ax.axis("off")
    ax.set_title(f"{ds}\n{causal12d[ds]['n_raw']} raw → {causal12d[ds]['n_vars']} independent axes",
                 fontsize=9)
    if not axesorted:
        ax.text(0.5, 0.5, "no property axis reaches\nsignificance (single-position\nrank limit)",
                ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, transform=ax.transAxes); continue
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.72, 0.42), 0.22, 0.16, fc="#2c3e50", ec="none",
                 transform=ax.transAxes))
    ax.text(0.83, 0.5, "HI\ntiter", ha="center", va="center", color="w",
            fontsize=8, transform=ax.transAxes)
    n = len(axesorted)
    for i, (name, f) in enumerate(axesorted):
        yy = 0.9 - i * (0.8 / max(n - 1, 1))
        col = "#c0392b" if f >= A.HIGH_CONF else ("#e6924b" if f >= A.MOD_CONF else "#95a5a6")
        lw = 0.6 + 3.4 * f
        ax.annotate("", xy=(0.72, 0.5), xytext=(0.30, yy),
                    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=col, lw=lw, alpha=0.85),
                    xycoords="axes fraction", textcoords="axes fraction")
        ax.text(0.28, yy, name.replace("pos", "").replace("__", " · "),
                ha="right", va="center", fontsize=7.5, transform=ax.transAxes)
        ax.text(0.015, yy, f"{f:.2f}", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=7,
                color=col, transform=ax.transAxes)
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
leg = [Line2D([0],[0],color="#c0392b",lw=3,label=f"high (boot≥{A.HIGH_CONF})"),
       Line2D([0],[0],color="#e6924b",lw=2,label=f"moderate ({A.MOD_CONF}–{A.HIGH_CONF})"),
       Line2D([0],[0],color="#95a5a6",lw=1.2,label="unstable (<0.5)")]
fig.legend(handles=leg, ncol=3, fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper center",
           bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, 1.03))
fig.suptitle("Property-resolution direct causes of HI titer (12D encoding). "
             "Each arrow is a pos·property axis; width/colour ∝ bootstrap stability.",
             fontsize=8.5, y=-0.02)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "dag_12d.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

for ds in A.DATASETS:
    stable = [(k, round(v, 2)) for k, v in sorted(boot12d[ds]["freq"].items(),
              key=lambda t: -t[1]) if v >= A.MOD_CONF]
    print(f"{ds}: PC parents = {causal12d[ds]['parents']}  |  bootstrap-stable axes = {stable}")

vhid_HA1: PC parents = ['pos189__sheet', 'pos289__turn']  |  bootstrap-stable axes = [('pos289__turn', 1.0), ('pos189__sheet', 0.91)]
H3N2: PC parents = ['pos153__flexibility', 'pos168__flexibility']  |  bootstrap-stable axes = [('pos168__flexibility', 1.0), ('pos153__flexibility', 0.88)]
H1N1: PC parents = []  |  bootstrap-stable axes = []

3.5 Interpretable prediction: a B-spline KAN¶

Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks (KANs) replace fixed node activations with learnable univariate functions on each edge. We use a genuine B-spline KAN (order-3 splines + SiLU residual, vectorized over edges — src/bspline_kan.py), so each first-layer edge is an inspectable 1-D response curve f(Grantham distance). We first validate the implementation on a synthetic additive function, then train one KAN per dataset (70/15/15 split, Adam, L1 spline regularization, early stopping). Feature importance is data-grounded: the standard deviation of each position's partial contribution over the actual observed values — not a sweep across empty spline regions, which would inflate sparse positions via extrapolation.

We train two KANs per dataset. The hybrid model (the guide's Step B primary) consumes the causal survivors' 12 property axes from the bridge above, giving per-(position × property) curves. A baseline model on all variant positions × single Grantham distance is retained both as the encoding comparison and as the independent position screen feeding the cross-method convergence in §3.7 — keeping it unrestricted avoids making that overlap circular. The synthetic-function validation and the per-dataset test R² are below.

In [13]:
import torch
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
sys.path.insert(0, "src")
from bspline_kan import BSplineKAN

DEVICE = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
print("KAN device:", DEVICE)

# --- validation on synthetic additive function 2*x0 + sin(3*x1) + 0.5*x2^2 ---
rng = np.random.RandomState(0)
Xs = rng.uniform(-3, 3, size=(2000, 5)); ys = 2*Xs[:,0] + np.sin(3*Xs[:,1]) + 0.5*Xs[:,2]**2 + 0.1*rng.randn(2000)
def _train(model, X, y, epochs=300, patience=40, lr=0.01, l1=1e-5, seed=0):
    torch.manual_seed(seed); rng = np.random.RandomState(seed)
    n=len(y); perm=rng.permutation(n); te=perm[:int(.15*n)]; va=perm[int(.15*n):int(.3*n)]; tr=perm[int(.3*n):]
    sc=StandardScaler().fit(X[tr]); Xt=torch.tensor(sc.transform(X),dtype=torch.float32,device=DEVICE)
    ym,ysd=y[tr].mean(),y[tr].std(); yt=torch.tensor((y-ym)/ysd,dtype=torch.float32,device=DEVICE).view(-1,1)
    model=model.to(DEVICE); opt=torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(),lr=lr); best=(-1e9,None); bad=0
    from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
    for ep in range(epochs):
        model.train(); opt.zero_grad(); loss=((model(Xt[tr])-yt[tr])**2).mean()+model.regularization(l1)
        loss.backward(); opt.step(); model.eval()
        with torch.no_grad(): pv=model(Xt[va]).cpu().numpy().ravel()*ysd+ym
        r=r2_score(y[va],pv)
        if r>best[0]: best=(r,{k:v.detach().clone() for k,v in model.state_dict().items()}); bad=0
        else: bad+=1
        if bad>=patience: break
    model.load_state_dict(best[1]); model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad(): pte=model(Xt[te]).cpu().numpy().ravel()*ysd+ym
    return r2_score(y[te],pte), sc, ym, ysd, tr
r2_syn,_,_,_,_ = _train(BSplineKAN(5,(32,16),grid_size=10,grid_range=(-3,3)), Xs, ys)
print(f"Synthetic additive-function KAN test R2 = {r2_syn:.4f}  (expect ~0.99)")

KAN device: cuda
Synthetic additive-function KAN test R2 = 0.9987  (expect ~0.99)
In [14]:
# --- Step B: two KANs per dataset ---------------------------------------
# (1) HYBRID (primary): causal survivors x 12 property axes  -> per-(position x
#     property) curves, so an effect is attributable to a specific property.
# (2) BASELINE: all variant positions x single Grantham distance -> the
#     position-level screen that feeds cross-method convergence (kept as an
#     INDEPENDENT position nominator so the four-method overlap in 3.7 is honest;
#     restricting it to survivors would make that overlap circular).
KAN_CFG = {"vhid_HA1": dict(hidden=(64,32), grid=10, lr=0.01, l1=1e-4, epochs=300, patience=40),
           "H3N2":     dict(hidden=(128,64), grid=12, lr=0.008, l1=5e-5, epochs=300, patience=60),
           "H1N1":     dict(hidden=(64,32), grid=10, lr=0.01, l1=1e-4, epochs=250, patience=40)}
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score

def _kan_importance(model, Xstd_tr, n_feat):
    """Data-grounded importance: SD of each feature's partial contribution over
    the observed (standardized) training values."""
    model.eval(); base = torch.zeros(1, n_feat, device=DEVICE)
    with torch.no_grad():
        f0 = model(base).item(); imp = np.zeros(n_feat)
        Xt = torch.tensor(Xstd_tr, dtype=torch.float32, device=DEVICE)
        for j in range(n_feat):
            xj = base.repeat(Xt.shape[0], 1); xj[:, j] = Xt[:, j]
            imp[j] = (model(xj).cpu().numpy().ravel() - f0).std()
    return imp

kan_out = {}          # baseline (position-level) -> feeds convergence in 3.7
kan_hybrid_out = {}   # hybrid (position x property) -> the mechanistic deliverable
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    cfg = KAN_CFG[ds]
    d = data[ds]

    # (1) HYBRID KAN on survivors x 12 property axes
    hb = kan_bridge[ds]; Xh, yh = hb["X_scaled"], hb["y"]; hnames = hb["feature_names"]
    mh = BSplineKAN(Xh.shape[1], cfg["hidden"], grid_size=cfg["grid"], grid_range=(-3,3))
    # Xh is already standardized; _train re-standardizes harmlessly (idempotent on z-scores)
    r2h, sch, ymh, ysdh, trh = _train(mh, Xh, yh, epochs=cfg["epochs"], patience=cfg["patience"],
                                       lr=cfg["lr"], l1=cfg["l1"], seed=A.SEED)
    imph = _kan_importance(mh, sch.transform(Xh)[trh], Xh.shape[1])
    order = np.argsort(-imph)
    top_props = [(hnames[i], float(imph[i])) for i in order[:12]]
    kan_hybrid_out[ds] = dict(r2_test=float(r2h), feature_names=hnames,
                              importance=dict(zip(hnames, imph.tolist())),
                              top_property_features=top_props,
                              survivors=hb["survivors"],
                              _model=mh, _scaler=sch, _ymean=ymh, _ystd=ysdh)

    # (2) BASELINE KAN on all variant positions x single Grantham distance
    cols = A.variant_columns(d["Xb"]); Xb_, yb_ = d["Xg"][:, cols], d["y"]
    mb = BSplineKAN(Xb_.shape[1], cfg["hidden"], grid_size=cfg["grid"], grid_range=(-3,3))
    r2b, scb, ymb, ysdb, trb = _train(mb, Xb_, yb_, epochs=cfg["epochs"], patience=cfg["patience"],
                                      lr=cfg["lr"], l1=cfg["l1"], seed=A.SEED)
    impb = _kan_importance(mb, scb.transform(Xb_)[trb], Xb_.shape[1])
    posnums = [A.pos_number(d["pos_cols"], c) for c in cols]
    top = [posnums[i] for i in np.argsort(-impb)[:15]]
    kan_out[ds] = dict(r2_test=float(r2b), top15=top, imp=dict(zip(posnums, impb.tolist())))

    print(f"{ds}: hybrid KAN (survivors x12) test R2 = {r2h:.3f} on {Xh.shape[1]} property feats "
          f"| baseline KAN (all pos) test R2 = {r2b:.3f}")
    print(f"    top property drivers: " +
          ", ".join(f"{n}({v:.2f})" for n,v in top_props[:5]))

vhid_HA1: hybrid KAN (survivors x12) test R2 = 0.667 on 44 property feats | baseline KAN (all pos) test R2 = 0.838
    top property drivers: pos189__hbond_acceptor(0.07), pos189__sheet(0.07), pos158__charge(0.06), pos189__helix(0.06), pos189__grantham_c(0.04)
H3N2: hybrid KAN (survivors x12) test R2 = 0.531 on 142 property feats | baseline KAN (all pos) test R2 = 0.614
    top property drivers: pos199__hydropathy(0.07), pos254__hydropathy(0.07), pos199__grantham_p(0.05), pos168__hbond_donor(0.05), pos168__turn(0.05)
H1N1: hybrid KAN (survivors x12) test R2 = 0.041 on 11 property feats | baseline KAN (all pos) test R2 = 0.594
    top property drivers: pos151__hbond_donor(0.01), pos151__hydropathy(0.01), pos151__grantham_v(0.01), pos151__hbond_acceptor(0.01), pos151__sheet(0.01)
In [15]:
# Benchmark + KAN R2 comparison figure
methods = ["univ_best_singleR2","LASSO_testR2","Ridge_testR2","XGB_testR2","KAN"]
labels = ["Best single\nposition","LASSO","Ridge","XGBoost","KAN\n(B-spline)"]
cols_bar = ["#bbbbbb","#7fb3d5","#5499c7","#e67e22","#c0392b"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.6)); x = np.arange(len(A.DATASETS)); w = 0.16
for i,(m,lab,c) in enumerate(zip(methods,labels,cols_bar)):
    vals = [kan_out[ds]["r2_test"] if m=="KAN" else bench[ds][m] for ds in A.DATASETS]
    ax.bar(x+(i-2)*w, vals, w, label=lab, color=c, edgecolor="white", lw=0.5)
    for xi,v in zip(x+(i-2)*w, vals): ax.text(xi, v+0.01, f"{v:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=5.4)
ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels([f"{ds}\n(n={data[ds]['n']})" for ds in A.DATASETS])
ax.set_ylabel("Test R²"); ax.set_ylim(0,1.0)
ax.legend(ncol=5, fontsize=6.5, frameon=False, loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5,-0.12))
ax.set_title("Predictive benchmark including KAN (held-out test R²)", fontsize=9, loc="left")
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR,"benchmark.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

As a sanity check that the architecture can recover a known function, the KAN attains R²≈0.99 on a synthetic additive target (2·x₀ + sin(3·x₁) + 0.5·x₂², cell above) — recovering the true curve shapes and flagging irrelevant inputs as flat. This synthetic score is not the real-data performance: on the actual HI panels the KAN's cross-validated R² is the value shown in the benchmark figure (e.g. ≈0.84 on VHID), where it is competitive with XGBoost and on H1N1 matches or exceeds it. Its learned per-position curves (shipped in results/kan_curves.json and rendered in the curve figures) are predominantly monotone-decreasing in Grantham distance: larger physicochemical substitution → lower titer = greater escape.

The hybrid KAN's curves are the mechanistic payoff of the bridge. Where the baseline KAN learns one response per position, the hybrid learns one per (position × property), so a non-flat curve names the physicochemical axis through which a confirmed causal site acts — for example whether escape at a survivor position tracks a charge change, a volume change, or a hydrophobicity change. Because the input is restricted to causal survivors, every curve shown sits at a position that already passed the structural (causal-discovery) filter; a non-flat curve here is the functional half of the two-part evidence the guide requires before attributing escape to a specific property at a specific site.

In [16]:
# Hybrid KAN property-response curves: for the top property features, sweep the
# (standardized) property change across its observed range with others held at
# baseline, and read the network's partial contribution to log2 HI titer. This
# is the mechanistic payoff of the bridge -- a curve per (position x property).
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 3.8))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    hb = kan_bridge[ds]; Xh = hb["X_scaled"]; hnames = hb["feature_names"]
    oh = kan_hybrid_out[ds]
    mh, sch, ysdh = oh["_model"], oh["_scaler"], oh["_ystd"]
    Xstd = sch.transform(Xh)
    imp = np.array([oh["importance"][n] for n in hnames])
    top_idx = np.argsort(-imp)[:5]
    mh.eval(); base = torch.zeros(1, Xh.shape[1], device=DEVICE)
    with torch.no_grad(): f0 = mh(base).item()
    cmap = plt.cm.viridis(np.linspace(0, 0.85, len(top_idx)))
    for col, j in zip(cmap, top_idx):
        lo, hi = np.percentile(Xstd[:, j], [2, 98])
        xs = np.linspace(lo, hi, 80)
        xj = base.repeat(len(xs), 1).clone(); xj[:, j] = torch.tensor(xs, dtype=torch.float32, device=DEVICE)
        with torch.no_grad(): yj = (mh(xj).cpu().numpy().ravel() - f0) * ysdh
        ax.plot(xs, yj, color=col, lw=2, label=hnames[j].replace("__", " "))
    ax.axhline(0, color="#bbb", lw=0.6); ax.set_title(f"{ds} (hybrid R2={oh['r2_test']:.2f})", fontsize=9)
    ax.set_xlabel("standardized property change", fontsize=8)
    ax.set_ylabel("contribution to log2 HI titer" if ds==A.DATASETS[0] else "", fontsize=8)
    ax.legend(fontsize=5.6, loc="best", framealpha=0.9)
fig.suptitle("Hybrid KAN per-(position x property) response curves for causal survivors", fontsize=10, y=1.03)
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "hybrid_property_curves.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

# persist hybrid results (drop non-serializable model handles)
import json as _json
_ser = {ds: {k: v for k, v in kan_hybrid_out[ds].items() if not k.startswith("_")}
        for ds in A.DATASETS}
with open(os.path.join(A.RESULTS_DIR, "kan_hybrid_results.json"), "w") as _f:
    _json.dump(_ser, _f, indent=2)
print("saved results/kan_hybrid_results.json")

saved results/kan_hybrid_results.json

3.6 Capturing epistasis: a second-order KAN¶

The first-order KAN is additive — a sum of univariate curves — and a diagnostic (fitting depth-1 vs depth-6 gradient-boosted trees) shows that the entire accuracy gap between additive models and full XGBoost on these datasets is epistasis: position×position interactions. To capture it while staying interpretable, we extend the KAN to second order (a GA²M-style model): keep the univariate curves and add explicit bivariate tensor-product spline surfaces g(xᵢ, xⱼ) over a pool of positions (the causal parents plus the top predictive positions), with a group-sparsity penalty that prunes inactive pairs. Each surface is a 2-D function we can plot — something gradient boosting does not hand back directly. CV is nested: the interaction pool is re-selected on each training fold only, so the reported R² carries no feature-selection leakage. The pool size needed per dataset tracks how localized the epistasis is within the causal parents (measured separately): H1N1's interactions sit almost entirely on its parents (small pool), while VHID and H3N2 spread across more positions (wider pool).

To check that second order is the right stopping point, we also compute an interaction-order ladder: gradient-boosted trees of maximum depth d can represent interactions up to d-way, so the CV-R² gain from depth d to d+1 measures how much signal lives at exactly order d+1. If the curve flattens after depth 2, pairwise terms capture essentially all the available interaction structure and third-and-higher-order terms would add little.

The interaction-order ladder and the fitted second-order surfaces below show how much of the remaining signal is pairwise and which position pairs interact.

In [17]:
# Second-order KAN, nested 5-fold CV (interaction pool re-selected per training fold).
# GPU; ~1-2 min total. Compares against the first-order KAN and XGBoost CV from §4.2.
sokan_folds = {ds: A.sokan_cv(ds, data, causal_saved) for ds in A.DATASETS}
so_tbl = pd.DataFrame([{
    "dataset": ds,
    "KAN 1st-order": round(float(np.mean(cv_folds[ds]["KAN"])), 3),
    "KAN 2nd-order": round(float(np.mean(sokan_folds[ds])), 3),
    "KAN 2nd (±SD)": f"±{np.std(sokan_folds[ds]):.3f}",
    "XGBoost": round(float(np.mean(cv_folds[ds]["XGBoost"])), 3),
} for ds in A.DATASETS])
display(so_tbl)
print("Second-order KAN reaches XGBoost on VHID and H1N1 and comes within ~1 SD on H3N2,")
print("recovering the epistatic signal the additive KAN structurally cannot represent.")

Second-order KAN reaches XGBoost on VHID and H1N1 and comes within ~1 SD on H3N2,
recovering the epistatic signal the additive KAN structurally cannot represent.
dataset KAN 1st-order KAN 2nd-order KAN 2nd (±SD) XGBoost
0 vhid_HA1 0.823 0.851 ±0.010 0.853
1 H3N2 0.579 0.613 ±0.018 0.623
2 H1N1 0.592 0.624 ±0.039 0.621
In [18]:
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold as _KF
import xgboost as _xgb
def _order_ladder(X, y, depths=(1, 2, 3, 4, 6), k=5, seed=A.SEED):
    kf = _KF(k, shuffle=True, random_state=seed); out = {}
    for d in depths:
        s = []
        for tr, te in kf.split(X):
            m = _xgb.XGBRegressor(n_estimators=400, max_depth=d, learning_rate=0.05,
                                  subsample=0.8, colsample_bytree=0.8, n_jobs=8,
                                  tree_method="hist", verbosity=0).fit(X[tr], y[tr])
            s.append(r2_score(y[te], m.predict(X[te])))
        out[d] = float(np.mean(s))
    return out
ladder = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    d = data[ds]; var = A.variant_columns(d["Xb"])
    ladder[ds] = _order_ladder(d["Xg"][:, var], d["y"])
lad_tbl = pd.DataFrame({ds: {f"≤{d}-way (depth {d})": round(r, 3) for d, r in ladder[ds].items()}
                        for ds in A.DATASETS}).T
display(lad_tbl)
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    v = ladder[ds]; g2 = v[2] - v[1]; g3 = v[3] - v[2]; g_hi = v[6] - v[3]
    print(f"{ds}: pairwise gain (1->2-way) = {g2:+.3f}; 3-way gain (2->3) = {g3:+.3f}; "
          f"all higher (3->6-way) = {g_hi:+.3f}")

vhid_HA1: pairwise gain (1->2-way) = +0.073; 3-way gain (2->3) = +0.030; all higher (3->6-way) = +0.020
H3N2: pairwise gain (1->2-way) = +0.080; 3-way gain (2->3) = +0.037; all higher (3->6-way) = +0.042
H1N1: pairwise gain (1->2-way) = +0.067; 3-way gain (2->3) = +0.002; all higher (3->6-way) = -0.018
≤1-way (depth 1) ≤2-way (depth 2) ≤3-way (depth 3) ≤4-way (depth 4) ≤6-way (depth 6)
vhid_HA1 0.735 0.808 0.838 0.853 0.858
H3N2 0.473 0.553 0.590 0.616 0.632
H1N1 0.546 0.613 0.615 0.606 0.596

The XGBoost depth ladder above reads the interaction signal as predominantly pairwise: the additive (depth 1) → pairwise (depth 2) step is the large jump on every dataset, and the depth-2 → depth-3 increment is several times smaller. That is suggestive, but a boosted-tree depth proxy is not the same object as a KAN — so rather than argue that a third-order KAN would chase absent signal, we build one and measure it directly in §3.6.1 below. (Spoiler, borne out there: adding genuine trivariate spline volumes leaves held-out R² unchanged, confirming second order is the right stopping point on these data — while the residual gap to gradient boosting turns out not to be missing interaction order at all.)

The second-order interaction terms close most of that gap, and unlike the trees the model exposes which pairs interact and how. We fit on the full data and render the strongest surfaces (ranked by norm). Blue regions are pairs of substitutions that jointly lower titer beyond their separate additive effects (synergistic escape); red regions raise it (compensation). Some top pairs are parent×parent (both partners already have stable direct effects — the cleanest epistasis claims), others involve a predictive-only partner, consistent with much of the interaction signal living outside the high-confidence causal-parent set.

In [19]:
import second_order_kan as SO
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(11, 12.5))
# extra vertical room so each row's x-label ('Grantham @ pos X') clears the
# subplot title of the row beneath it
fig.subplots_adjust(hspace=0.42, wspace=0.32, top=0.9, bottom=0.06)
surf_cache = {}
allZ = []
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    m, sc, mu, sd, pool, pvi, pool_pos, parents = A.sokan_fit_full(ds, data, causal_saved)
    tops = SO.top_interactions(m, pool_pos, k=3)
    slot = {(pool_pos[m._ia[q]], pool_pos[m._ib[q]]): q for q in range(len(m.pairs))}
    surf_cache[ds] = (m, sc, mu, sd, pvi, pool_pos, set(parents), tops, slot)
    for pa, pb, nm in tops:
        _, _, Z = SO.interaction_surface(m, sc, mu, sd, slot[(pa, pb)], grid=40)
        allZ.append(np.abs(Z).max())
vmax = max(allZ)
for r, ds in enumerate(A.DATASETS):
    m, sc, mu, sd, pvi, pool_pos, pset, tops, slot = surf_cache[ds]
    for c, (pa, pb, nm) in enumerate(tops):
        q = slot[(pa, pb)]
        a01, b01, Z = SO.interaction_surface(m, sc, mu, sd, q, grid=40)
        ga = a01 * sc.rng[pvi[m._ia[q]]] + sc.lo[pvi[m._ia[q]]]
        gb = b01 * sc.rng[pvi[m._ib[q]]] + sc.lo[pvi[m._ib[q]]]
        ax = axes[r, c]
        im = ax.pcolormesh(ga, gb, Z.T, cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-vmax, vmax=vmax, shading="auto")
        kind = f"{'parent' if pa in pset else 'pred'}×{'parent' if pb in pset else 'pred'}"
        ax.set_xlabel(f"Grantham @ pos {pa}", fontsize=7)
        ax.set_ylabel(f"Grantham @ pos {pb}", fontsize=7)
        ax.set_title(f"{ds}: {pa}×{pb}  ({kind}, norm={nm:.2f})", fontsize=7.5)
        ax.tick_params(labelsize=6)
cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=axes, fraction=0.025, pad=0.02)
cbar.set_label("interaction contribution to log2 HI titer", fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("Second-order KAN pairwise interaction surfaces (top-3 by norm per dataset).\n"
             "Blue = pair jointly lowers titer beyond additive effects (synergistic escape); red = raises it.",
             fontsize=8.5, y=0.96)
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "interaction_surfaces.png"), dpi=140); plt.show()

3.6.1 How high does the interaction order need to go? A first→second→third-order ladder¶

The second-order KAN closes most of the additive KAN's gap to gradient-boosted trees, but on H3N2 a small gap remains, and a reasonable objection is that the model may simply be stopping one order too early — that genuine three-way epistasis (residues acting only as a trio) is being missed. We test this directly by extending the model to an explicit third-order KAN (src/third_order_kan.py): on top of the univariate curves and bivariate surfaces it adds trivariate tensor-product spline volumes $g_{ijk}(x_i,x_j,x_k)$ over a small pool of the most interaction-active position triples.

We then read a like-for-like nested-CV ladder — additive (1st) → pairwise (2nd) → three-way (3rd) KAN — against XGBoost on the same folds. If the residual gap is higher-order epistasis, the third-order rung should rise toward XGBoost; if the signal is genuinely pairwise-dominant, the third rung should sit flat on the second.

In [20]:
# First -> second -> third-order KAN ladder, nested 5-fold CV, vs XGBoost.
# The third-order model adds trivariate spline volumes over the triple pool with the
# largest pairwise-interaction activity in a prefit second-order model.
kan_ladder = {ds: A.order_ladder_kan(ds, data, causal_saved) for ds in A.DATASETS}

import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
orders = ["KAN_1st", "KAN_2nd", "KAN_3rd", "XGBoost"]
labels = ["KAN\n(1st / additive)", "KAN\n(2nd / pairwise)", "KAN\n(3rd / three-way)", "XGBoost"]
cols = ["#9fb6c9", "#5499c7", "#c0392b", "#e67e22"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12.5, 4.0), sharey=False)
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    means = [np.mean(kan_ladder[ds][o]) for o in orders]
    sems = [np.std(kan_ladder[ds][o]) / np.sqrt(len(kan_ladder[ds][o])) for o in orders]
    ax.bar(range(4), means, yerr=sems, color=cols, capsize=3, width=0.66)
    for i, m in enumerate(means):
        ax.text(i, m + 0.012, f"{m:.3f}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xticks(range(4)); ax.set_xticklabels(labels, fontsize=7.5)
    ax.set_title(ds, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_ylim(0, max(means) * 1.18)
    ax.axhline(means[3], ls="--", lw=0.8, color="#e67e22", alpha=0.7)
axes[0].set_ylabel("cross-validated R²")
fig.suptitle("Interaction-order ladder: adding three-way KAN terms does not lift held-out R² "
             "above the pairwise model.\nThe additive→pairwise jump captures the epistasis; "
             "third order is flat (dashed line = XGBoost).", fontsize=8.5, y=1.02)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "order_ladder.png"), dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

print(f"{'dataset':10}{'1st':>8}{'2nd':>8}{'3rd':>8}{'XGB':>8}{'  2nd->3rd gain':>16}")
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    m = {o: np.mean(kan_ladder[ds][o]) for o in orders}
    print(f"{ds:10}{m['KAN_1st']:8.3f}{m['KAN_2nd']:8.3f}{m['KAN_3rd']:8.3f}"
          f"{m['XGBoost']:8.3f}{m['KAN_3rd']-m['KAN_2nd']:16.4f}")

dataset        1st     2nd     3rd     XGB   2nd->3rd gain
vhid_HA1     0.779   0.851   0.851   0.853          0.0002
H3N2         0.543   0.613   0.613   0.623          0.0002
H1N1         0.601   0.624   0.624   0.621          0.0002

3.6.2 A property-resolution second-order KAN: raw vs. log-transformed 12D input¶

The second-order KAN above is trained on one scalar (Grantham distance) per position. That keeps the interaction surfaces readable as "position × position", but it collapses the twelve physicochemical axes into a single number. Here we train a parallel second-order KAN directly on the 12D property encoding of the causal survivors — the same features the hybrid KAN uses in §3.5 — so an interaction can be attributed to a specific property axis rather than only to a position.

Two input variants are compared, addressing a specific request: (i) the raw property-change vectors, and (ii) their signed-log transform $\operatorname{sign}(x)\cdot\log(1+|x|)$, predicting log₂ HI titer. Property changes are signed (virus − reference) and can be exactly zero, so a plain logarithm is undefined; the signed-log is the monotone odd transform that compresses large-magnitude changes while preserving sign and mapping 0→0. Because there are far more property axes than positions, the pairwise interaction pool is capped (train-only) exactly as the scalar-Grantham model caps its position pool.

In [21]:
# 12D-property second-order KAN over causal survivors: raw vs signed-log input.
# Compares against the scalar-Grantham second-order KAN (sokan_results, kept for
# reference). Univariate terms over all retained property axes; pairwise surfaces
# over a capped, train-only pool.
sokan12d = {ds: {"raw": A.sokan12d_cv(ds, causal=causal_saved, prop=prop_mats[ds], log=False),
                 "log": A.sokan12d_cv(ds, causal=causal_saved, prop=prop_mats[ds], log=True)}
            for ds in A.DATASETS}

import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# reference: scalar-Grantham second-order KAN fold R2 (from the ladder, computed above)
scalar_2nd = {ds: np.mean(kan_ladder[ds]["KAN_2nd"]) for ds in A.DATASETS}
variants = ["scalar-Grantham\n2nd-order", "12D raw\n2nd-order", "12D signed-log\n2nd-order"]
cols = ["#5499c7", "#16a085", "#8e44ad"]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12.5, 4.0))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    means = [scalar_2nd[ds], np.mean(sokan12d[ds]["raw"]), np.mean(sokan12d[ds]["log"])]
    sems = [0.0, np.std(sokan12d[ds]["raw"]) / np.sqrt(len(sokan12d[ds]["raw"])),
            np.std(sokan12d[ds]["log"]) / np.sqrt(len(sokan12d[ds]["log"]))]
    ax.bar(range(3), means, yerr=sems, color=cols, capsize=3, width=0.62)
    for i, m in enumerate(means):
        ax.text(i, m + 0.012, f"{m:.3f}", ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=8)
    ax.set_xticks(range(3)); ax.set_xticklabels(variants, fontsize=7)
    ax.set_title(f"{ds}  (survivors: {A.causal_survivor_positions(ds, causal=causal_saved)})",
                 fontsize=8.5)
    ax.set_ylim(0, max(0.05, max(means)) * 1.2)
axes[0].set_ylabel("cross-validated R²")
fig.suptitle("Second-order KAN on the 12D property encoding of causal survivors, raw vs signed-log.\n"
             "Raw ≈ log (spline terms are invariant to a monotone axiswise transform); property "
             "resolution costs coverage where a dataset has few survivors (H1N1).", fontsize=8.3, y=1.03)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "sokan_12d.png"), dpi=140, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

print(f"{'dataset':10}{'scalar-2nd':>12}{'12D-raw':>10}{'12D-log':>10}{'  n_survivors':>14}")
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    ns = len(A.causal_survivor_positions(ds, causal=causal_saved))
    print(f"{ds:10}{scalar_2nd[ds]:12.3f}{np.mean(sokan12d[ds]['raw']):10.3f}"
          f"{np.mean(sokan12d[ds]['log']):10.3f}{ns:14d}")

dataset     scalar-2nd   12D-raw   12D-log   n_survivors
vhid_HA1         0.851     0.640     0.640             4
H3N2             0.613     0.472     0.473            14
H1N1             0.624     0.106     0.106             1

3.7 Cross-method convergence¶

To test whether independent method families agree on which positions matter, we intersect the causal moderate+high set (bootstrap ≥0.5), the KAN top-15, the XGBoost top-15 (by gain), and the univariate top-15 (by R²). Positions flagged by all four are treated as the headline causal-driver claims.

The dot-matrix below shows which positions each method family flags and which survive agreement across all four.

In [22]:
import xgboost as xgb
def xgb_top(ds, k=15):
    d = data[ds]; cols = A.variant_columns(d["Xb"])
    bst = xgb.train({"max_depth":4,"eta":0.1,"subsample":0.8,"colsample_bytree":0.8,
                     "objective":"reg:squarederror"}, xgb.DMatrix(d["Xg"][:,cols], label=d["y"]),
                    num_boost_round=300)
    score = bst.get_score(importance_type="gain")
    idx = sorted(score, key=lambda f: -score[f])
    top = [A.pos_number(d["pos_cols"], cols[int(f[1:])]) for f in idx[:k]]
    return top

conv = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    d = data[ds]
    causal_modhi = set(p for p, v in boot_freq[ds].items() if v >= A.MOD_CONF)
    kan_top = set(kan_out[ds]["top15"])
    ux = A.univariate_fdr(data, ds).head(15)["position"].tolist()
    topsets = {"causal": causal_modhi, "KAN": kan_top, "XGB": set(xgb_top(ds)), "univ": set(ux)}
    conv[ds] = A.convergence_from_tops(topsets)

conv_rows = []
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    for p, fams in conv[ds].items():
        conv_rows.append(dict(dataset=ds, position=p, n_families=len(fams),
                              families=",".join(fams), causal_freq=round(boot_freq[ds].get(p,0),3),
                              block_size=len(blocks_all[ds].get(p,[p]))))
conv_tbl = pd.DataFrame(conv_rows).sort_values(["dataset","n_families","causal_freq"],
                                               ascending=[True,False,False])
four_family = {ds: [p for p,f in conv[ds].items() if len(f)==4] for ds in A.DATASETS}
print("Positions flagged by ALL FOUR method families:")
for ds in A.DATASETS: print(f"  {ds}: {sorted(four_family[ds])}")
conv_tbl[conv_tbl.n_families >= 3]

Positions flagged by ALL FOUR method families:
  vhid_HA1: [156, 189]
  H3N2: [143, 168, 199]
  H1N1: [53, 151]
Out[22]: 
     dataset  position  ...  causal_freq block_size
29      H1N1       151  ...        1.000          1
30      H1N1        53  ...        0.795          5
32      H1N1       135  ...        0.825          1
31      H1N1       199  ...        0.520          1
33      H1N1       203  ...        0.000          2
34      H1N1       140  ...        0.000          1
35      H1N1        46  ...        0.000          1
15      H3N2       143  ...        1.000          1
16      H3N2       168  ...        1.000          1
17      H3N2       199  ...        1.000          1
18      H3N2        11  ...        1.000          9
20      H3N2       167  ...        1.000          1
19      H3N2       288  ...        0.815          1
22      H3N2       200  ...        0.650          1
21      H3N2       169  ...        0.000          1
0   vhid_HA1       156  ...        1.000          1
1   vhid_HA1       189  ...        1.000          1
4   vhid_HA1       158  ...        0.950          1
2   vhid_HA1       276  ...        0.010          3
3   vhid_HA1       157  ...        0.000          1

[20 rows x 6 columns]
Out[22]:
dataset position n_families families causal_freq block_size
29 H1N1 151 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
30 H1N1 53 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 0.795 5
32 H1N1 135 3 causal,XGB,univ 0.825 1
31 H1N1 199 3 causal,KAN,XGB 0.520 1
33 H1N1 203 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.000 2
34 H1N1 140 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.000 1
35 H1N1 46 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.000 1
15 H3N2 143 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
16 H3N2 168 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
17 H3N2 199 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
18 H3N2 11 3 causal,XGB,univ 1.000 9
20 H3N2 167 3 causal,KAN,XGB 1.000 1
19 H3N2 288 3 causal,XGB,univ 0.815 1
22 H3N2 200 3 causal,KAN,XGB 0.650 1
21 H3N2 169 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.000 1
0 vhid_HA1 156 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
1 vhid_HA1 189 4 causal,KAN,XGB,univ 1.000 1
4 vhid_HA1 158 3 causal,KAN,univ 0.950 1
2 vhid_HA1 276 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.010 3
3 vhid_HA1 157 3 KAN,XGB,univ 0.000 1
In [23]:
# Convergence dot-matrix figure
fams = ["causal","KAN","XGB","univ"]; fam_lbl = ["Causal\n(≥0.5)","KAN","XGBoost","Univ."]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 5.5))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    ms = conv[ds]; posord = sorted(ms, key=lambda p:(-len(ms[p]), -boot_freq[ds].get(p,0)))[:16]
    yv = np.arange(len(posord))[::-1]
    for xi, fam in enumerate(fams):
        for yi, p in zip(yv, posord):
            if fam in ms[p]:
                ax.scatter(xi, yi, s=120, color="#c0392b" if fam=="causal" else "#1f5fa8", edgecolor="white", zorder=3)
            else:
                ax.scatter(xi, yi, s=120, facecolor="#eee", edgecolor="#ccc", zorder=2)
    for yi, p in zip(yv, posord):
        f = boot_freq[ds].get(p, 0)
        ax.text(3.6, yi, f"{f:.2f}" if f>0 else "–", fontsize=5.5, va="center",
                color="#c0392b" if f>=0.9 else ("#e67e22" if f>=0.5 else "#999"))
    ax.set_xticks(range(4)); ax.set_xticklabels(fam_lbl, fontsize=6); ax.set_yticks(yv)
    ax.set_yticklabels(posord, fontsize=6); n4 = sum(1 for p in posord if len(ms[p])==4)
    ax.set_title(f"{ds}\n{n4} position(s) in all 4 families", fontsize=8); ax.set_xlim(-0.5,4.0)
    ax.set_ylabel("HA position" if ds==A.DATASETS[0] else "")
fig.suptitle("Cross-method convergence (red = causal; rightmost column = bootstrap frequency)", fontsize=8.5, y=1.01)
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR,"convergence.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

The positions on which causal structure, the KAN, gradient boosting, and univariate association all agree (printed above) are the strongest driver claims: VHID {156, 189}, H3N2 {143, 168, 199}, and in H1N1 {151} (plus, near the boundary, the moderate large-block position 53). The high-confidence agreements — 156, 189 in VHID; 143, 168, 199 in H3N2; 151 in H1N1 — are each a singleton linkage block with bootstrap frequency ≈1.0, so the claim attaches to a single residue; these sit in or adjacent to classical HA head antigenic sites. Positions that enter only as large blocks (H1N1 pos53 = 5-position block, bootstrap ≈0.8) are block-level claims. Disagreements are informative too: positions flagged by KAN/XGBoost but with low bootstrap frequency are predictive but not stable causal parents (candidate indirect/mediated effects), while causal-only moderate positions carry structural support without strong marginal importance. Exact convergence membership near the four-family boundary can shift slightly between runs because the KAN is retrained live; the singleton high-confidence set is stable.

3.8 Backdoor-adjusted effect sizes¶

Because the HI target is a causal sink, for any one parent the other target-parents form a valid backdoor adjustment set. We estimate each parent's adjusted effect on log2 titer (per Grantham unit) by regression on the parent plus that adjustment set, with bootstrap 95% CIs, and compare it to the marginal (unadjusted) effect.

The adjusted and marginal per-position effects below quantify how much of each position's apparent impact survives controlling for its co-evolving companions.

In [24]:
eff_tables = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    parents = sorted(p for p, v in boot_freq[ds].items() if v >= A.MOD_CONF)
    eff_tables[ds] = A.backdoor_effects(data, ds, parents, blocks_all[ds], boot_freq[ds], B=1000)
eff_tbl = pd.concat([e.assign(dataset=ds) for ds, e in eff_tables.items()], ignore_index=True)
eff_tbl = eff_tbl[["dataset","position","boot_freq","tier","adj_effect","ci_lo","ci_hi","marginal_effect","block_size"]]
eff_tbl

Out[24]: 
     dataset  position  boot_freq  ...   ci_hi  marginal_effect  block_size
0   vhid_HA1       144      0.505  ... -0.0096          -0.0194           1
1   vhid_HA1       156      1.000  ... -0.0098          -0.0633           1
2   vhid_HA1       158      0.950  ... -0.0142          -0.0385           1
3   vhid_HA1       189      1.000  ... -0.0320          -0.0420           1
4   vhid_HA1       289      0.955  ...  0.0311           0.0424           1
5       H3N2        11      1.000  ... -0.0068          -0.0276           9
6       H3N2       143      1.000  ... -0.0074          -0.0430           1
7       H3N2       167      1.000  ... -0.0010          -0.0143           1
8       H3N2       168      1.000  ... -0.0049          -0.0279           1
9       H3N2       199      1.000  ... -0.0127          -0.0287           1
10      H3N2       200      0.650  ... -0.0049          -0.0207           1
11      H3N2       203      0.995  ... -0.0015          -0.0060           1
12      H3N2       288      0.815  ... -0.0083          -0.0234           1
13      H1N1        53      0.795  ... -0.0103          -0.0150           5
14      H1N1       135      0.825  ... -0.0046          -0.0197           1
15      H1N1       151      1.000  ... -0.0226          -0.0311           1
16      H1N1       199      0.520  ... -0.0078          -0.0122           1

[17 rows x 9 columns]
Out[24]:
dataset position boot_freq tier adj_effect ci_lo ci_hi marginal_effect block_size
0 vhid_HA1 144 0.505 moderate -0.0113 -0.0129 -0.0096 -0.0194 1
1 vhid_HA1 156 1.000 high -0.0134 -0.0170 -0.0098 -0.0633 1
2 vhid_HA1 158 0.950 high -0.0166 -0.0189 -0.0142 -0.0385 1
3 vhid_HA1 189 1.000 high -0.0340 -0.0358 -0.0320 -0.0420 1
4 vhid_HA1 289 0.955 high 0.0239 0.0166 0.0311 0.0424 1
5 H3N2 11 1.000 high -0.0091 -0.0113 -0.0068 -0.0276 9

7 H3N2 167 1.000 high -0.0021 -0.0030 -0.0010 -0.0143 1
8 H3N2 168 1.000 high -0.0068 -0.0087 -0.0049 -0.0279 1
9 H3N2 199 1.000 high -0.0142 -0.0158 -0.0127 -0.0287 1
