11 H3N2 203 0.995 high -0.0022 -0.0029 -0.0015 -0.0060 1
12 H3N2 288 0.815 moderate -0.0096 -0.0109 -0.0083 -0.0234 1
13 H1N1 53 0.795 moderate -0.0140 -0.0173 -0.0103 -0.0150 5
14 H1N1 135 0.825 moderate -0.0083 -0.0117 -0.0046 -0.0197 1
15 H1N1 151 1.000 high -0.0272 -0.0324 -0.0226 -0.0311 1
16 H1N1 199 0.520 moderate -0.0100 -0.0121 -0.0078 -0.0122 1
In [25]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12.5, 4.6))
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    sub = eff_tables[ds].sort_values("adj_effect"); yv = np.arange(len(sub))
    for yi,(_,r) in zip(yv, sub.iterrows()):
        c = "#c0392b" if r["tier"]=="high" else "#e6924b"
        ax.errorbar(r["adj_effect"], yi, xerr=[[r["adj_effect"]-r["ci_lo"]],[r["ci_hi"]-r["adj_effect"]]],
                    fmt="o", color=c, capsize=3, ms=6, mec="white", zorder=3)
        ax.scatter(r["marginal_effect"], yi, marker="x", color="#999", s=28, zorder=2)
    ax.axvline(0, color="#333", lw=0.8, ls="--"); ax.set_yticks(yv)
    ax.set_yticklabels([f"{r['position']}{'*' if r['block_size']>1 else ''}" for _,r in sub.iterrows()], fontsize=7.5)
    ax.set_title(ds, fontsize=9); ax.set_xlabel("Effect on log2 HI titer per Grantham unit")
    ax.grid(axis="x", ls=":", lw=0.5, alpha=0.5)
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
leg = [Line2D([0],[0],marker="o",color="w",mfc="#c0392b",ms=7,label="High-conf, adjusted ±95% CI"),
       Line2D([0],[0],marker="o",color="w",mfc="#e6924b",ms=7,label="Moderate"),
       Line2D([0],[0],marker="x",color="#999",ms=7,lw=0,label="Marginal (unadjusted)")]
fig.legend(handles=leg, ncol=3, fontsize=7, frameon=False, loc="upper center", bbox_to_anchor=(0.5,1.02), columnspacing=3.0)
fig.suptitle("Backdoor-adjusted per-position causal effects. * = multi-position linkage block.", fontsize=8, y=1.09)
fig.tight_layout(); fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR,"effect_sizes.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()

The adjusted effects are all significant (bootstrap CI excludes zero) and systematically smaller in magnitude than the marginal effects — the ×'s sit farther from zero than the dots — directly quantifying the phylogenetic confounding that backdoor adjustment removes. Adjustment shrinks every effect toward zero but never flips its sign: all 17 parents keep the same sign marginally and adjusted. Almost all effects are negative (larger physicochemical substitution → lower titer = greater escape). The one exception is VHID pos289, which is positive in both the marginal (+0.042) and adjusted (+0.024) estimates — larger substitution there associates with higher cross-titer, the opposite of an escape signature, consistent with a compensatory or stabilizing role rather than direct epitope escape.

3.9 Validating the causal structure¶

A discovered DAG is a hypothesis, and no single test certifies it, so we run four complementary correctness tests on the titer-sink graph (implemented in src/dag_validation.py), each answering a different question:

  1. Goodness-of-fit (Shipley d-separation basis-set test) — is the DAG contradicted by the data's conditional independences? Falsification only, at the equivalence-class level.
  2. Bootstrap stability — how reproducible is each edge under resampling, aggregated to the linkage-group level? This is a separate, stronger computation from the edge frequencies labeling the DAG figure in §3.4: there, the numbers are the original PC/GES discovery bootstrap (bootstrap_freq); here, we re-run an independent partial-correlation parent-selection pipeline on each resample and aggregate to linkage groups, so the two sets of frequencies are not expected to be identical.
  3. Direct-effect estimates — how strong is each retained direct cause, with honest CIs?
  4. Out-of-distribution transport (leave-one-era-out) — does the parent set hold when the strain background changes? This is the only one of the four that probes causal content as such.

The four tests are reported together below.

In [26]:
import dag_validation as V

candidates = {ds: sorted(p for p, v in boot_freq[ds].items() if v >= A.MOD_CONF)
              for ds in A.DATASETS}
val = {}
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    vdf = A.build_validation_frame(data, ds, candidates[ds])
    feat = vdf.drop(columns=["era"])
    pdict = V.parents_from_edges([(f"pos{p}", "titer") for p in candidates[ds]])
    pipe = A.make_parent_pipeline(candidates[ds])
    cg = A.collapse_groups_map(ds, blocks_all[ds], candidates[ds])
    gof = V.dsep_basis_test(pdict, feat)
    stab = V.bootstrap_stability(pipe, feat, B=200, collapse_groups=cg)
    eff = V.effect_estimates(feat, target="titer",
                             parents=[f"pos{p}" for p in candidates[ds]], split=False)
    dfo = vdf[vdf["era"] != "NA"].reset_index(drop=True)
    ood = V.ood_validation(dfo, group_col="era", discovery_fn=pipe, target="titer")
    val[ds] = dict(gof=gof, stab=stab, eff=eff, ood=ood)
    print(f"{ds}: GOF p={gof['p_overall']:.3g} ({'REJECTED' if gof['rejected_at_alpha'] else 'not rejected'}); "
          f"stable groups (≥0.9)={sum(1 for f in stab['group_freq'].values() if f>=0.9)}; "
          f"OOD R² range {min(r['r2'] for r in ood['per_group']):.2f}–{max(r['r2'] for r in ood['per_group']):.2f}")

vhid_HA1: GOF p=0 (REJECTED); stable groups (≥0.9)=5; OOD R² range 0.45–0.75
H3N2: GOF p=0 (REJECTED); stable groups (≥0.9)=8; OOD R² range -0.15–0.63
H1N1: GOF p=0 (REJECTED); stable groups (≥0.9)=4; OOD R² range -0.16–0.44
In [27]:
# Bootstrap group-stability + OOD transport, as tables per dataset
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    gf = sorted(val[ds]["stab"]["group_freq"].items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1])
    st = pd.DataFrame([{"group": g.replace("->","→"), "bootstrap_freq": round(f,3)}
                       for (g, _), f in gf])
    print(f"\n=== {ds}: edge bootstrap stability (linkage-group level) ===")
    display(st)
    od = pd.DataFrame(val[ds]["ood"]["per_group"])
    od = od[["held_out_group","n_test","rmse","r2","parent_recovery_jaccard","n_parents"]].round(3)
    print(f"--- {ds}: leave-one-era-out OOD transport ---")
    display(od)

In [28]:
# OOD transport figure: held-out-era R² and parent-set recovery per dataset
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(13, 4), sharey=True)
for ax, ds in zip(axes, A.DATASETS):
    od = pd.DataFrame(val[ds]["ood"]["per_group"]).sort_values("held_out_group")
    x = np.arange(len(od))
    ax.bar(x, od["r2"], color="#5499c7", edgecolor="white", label="held-out R²")
    ax.plot(x, od["parent_recovery_jaccard"], "o-", color="#c0392b", ms=5,
            lw=1.5, label="parent recovery (Jaccard)")
    ax.set_xticks(x); ax.set_xticklabels(od["held_out_group"], rotation=45, fontsize=6)
    ax.set_title(ds, fontsize=9); ax.set_ylim(-0.35, 1.05)
    ax.axhline(0, color="#888", lw=0.7, ls="-")
    ax.set_xlabel("held-out era"); ax.grid(axis="y", ls=":", lw=0.5, alpha=0.5)
    if ds == A.DATASETS[0]: ax.set_ylabel("R² / Jaccard")
axes[-1].legend(fontsize=6.5, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.suptitle("Out-of-distribution transport: discover+fit on all-but-one era, predict the held-out era. "
             "High R² + Jaccard≈1 = the parent set is not era-specific.", fontsize=7.3, y=1.02)
fig.tight_layout()
fig.savefig(os.path.join(A.FIG_DIR, "ood_transport.png"), dpi=130, bbox_inches="tight"); plt.show()
In [29]:
# Assemble the four tests into one markdown report artifact
for ds in A.DATASETS:
    V.validation_report(gof=val[ds]["gof"], stability=val[ds]["stab"],
                        effects=val[ds]["eff"], ood=val[ds]["ood"],
                        path=os.path.join(A.RESULTS_DIR, f"validation_report_{ds}.md"))
print("Wrote validation_report_{vhid_HA1,H3N2,H1N1}.md to results/")

Wrote validation_report_{vhid_HA1,H3N2,H1N1}.md to results/

Reading the four tests together. The goodness-of-fit test rejects the pure sink-star (small p): the retained parents are not mutually independent given the graph, because they co-evolve along the shared phylogeny — the star encodes which loci are direct-cause candidates but not the residual among-parent dependence. This is an honest limitation, not a contradiction of the driver claims, and it is exactly why the other three tests matter. Bootstrap stability is high at the linkage-group level (the high-confidence groups recur in ≈100% of resamples). Direct-effect estimates reproduce the backdoor-adjusted magnitudes of Section 3.8.

The leave-one-era-out transport test separates two questions that a single R² would conflate. The identity of the drivers transports well: the parent set discovered on all-but-one collection era recovers the same positions on most held-out eras (Jaccard = 1.0 for the majority of folds), with a few late, most-drift-separated folds dropping to partial overlap (min 0.50 at H1N1's 2005 era; 0.60 at VHID's 1990 era; 0.75 at H3N2's 2005 era) — so the drivers are largely, though not perfectly, stable across time rather than era-specific hitchhiker correlations. Their quantitative effect magnitudes transport much less well: held-out predictive R² is strong for VHID (≈0.45–0.75) but degrades over time for Bedford H3N2 and H1N1, turning slightly negative on the largest, most drift-separated folds (e.g. the H3N2 2000s block). That degradation is the expected signature of antigenic drift — the causal skeleton is stable but the per-substitution effect sizes and titer baselines are non-stationary, so a model fit on other decades identifies the right positions yet mis-calibrates the level for a held-out decade. The stable skeleton is the strongest observational evidence available for the drivers' causal status; the drifting magnitudes are a caution against treating the effect sizes as time-invariant. Full per-dataset reports are in results/validation_report_*.md. OOD success at the structural level is necessary, not sufficient; the definitive test remains a mutagenesis intervention at a predicted direct cause.

4. Conclusion¶

We set out to separate the HA positions that cause antigenic drift from those that merely accompany it, using methods whose outputs a biologist can inspect and a modeler can trust. This closing section reads the results back against that goal: what the convergent drivers mean biologically (§4.1), where the data cannot support residue-level attribution (§4.2), which assumptions the causal claims rest on (§4.3), and where the work goes next (§4.4).

4.1 Biological interpretation of the causal results¶

The convergent, high-confidence causal drivers land squarely on the receptor-binding subunit's antigenic sites — the residues classical serology and structural biology already implicate in escape:

  • VHID / Bedford H3N2 — positions 156, 158, 189 (and 143, 167, 168, 199, 203). In H3 numbering these fall in and around antigenic sites B (near 156–160 and 188–198, adjacent to the receptor-binding site) and D. Position 189 in particular is a well-known determinant of H3N2 antigenic cluster transitions. That our purely data-driven causal pipeline recovers them from HI titers alone — without any structural prior beyond the sink orientation — is a strong external validation of the approach.
  • Bedford H1N1 — position 151. This sits in the vicinity of the H1 Sa/Sb head epitopes flanking the receptor-binding site. It is the single all-four-family driver for H1N1 and has the largest adjusted effect of any position in the study (≈ −0.027 log2 titer per Grantham unit).

The backdoor adjustment sharpens the interpretation: adjusted effects are much smaller than marginal ones, meaning a large part of each position's naive association is confounding shared with co-evolving sites. The framework thus does what pure prediction cannot — it estimates how much of a position's apparent antigenic impact survives after controlling for its phylogenetic companions.

Repeating the discovery at property resolution (§3.4.2) sharpens what kind of change each driver represents. Running the same target-oriented causal search on the 12-dimensional physicochemical encoding — rather than a single Grantham scalar per position — the bootstrap-stable direct-cause axes are pos189 · β-sheet propensity and pos289 · turn propensity in VHID H3N2, and pos153 · flexibility and pos168 · flexibility in Bedford H3N2. That the strongest property axes are backbone-conformational (sheet, turn, flexibility) rather than, say, charge or volume is consistent with escape at these sites acting through local mainchain geometry of the epitope loop rather than a simple electrostatic swap — a concrete, structurally testable hypothesis the scalar analysis cannot express. The interaction-order ladder (§3.6.1) closes a companion question: an explicit third-order KAN leaves held-out R² unchanged versus the pairwise model on all three datasets, so the residual gap to gradient boosting is not unmodeled higher-order epistasis — the escape landscape is genuinely additive-plus-pairwise, and the second-order KAN is at the right functional complexity.

4.2 Where attribution is limited¶

H3N2 is the cautionary case: its largest linkage block spans 88 co-evolving positions and a second spans 55. These absorb many head positions into single clade-linked units that no observational method can internally resolve — which is also why H3N2 has the lowest predictive ceiling. Claims for positions in large blocks (e.g. H3N2 pos11, H1N1 pos53) belong to the block, not the single residue. Resolving them would require denser sampling across genetic backgrounds that break the linkage.

4.3 Assumptions¶

All causal results are equivalence-class candidates, not a settled DAG: PC returns a CPDAG and FCI a PAG; the sink prior orients feature→target edges but leaves some among-feature orientations ambiguous. The CI tests use a Gaussian / Fisher-z working model (the discrete test is intractable at this scale), so purely nonlinear dependences can be missed by the constraint step — the KAN partially covers this gap on the predictive side. FCI ran only for VHID; PC assumes causal sufficiency, so unmeasured drivers (NA, glycosylation, non-HA1 regions) remain possible confounders. Discovery, mechanism, and effect estimate are distinct: a high-confidence parent is a direct-cause candidate, and confirming mechanism needs structural or experimental follow-up. The validation of Section 3.9 makes two of these caveats concrete: the d-separation test rejects the pure sink-star (the parents co-vary along the phylogeny, so the star is an incomplete structure), and the out-of-distribution test shows the effect magnitudes are non-stationary across collection eras even though the driver identities are not — so the reported effect sizes should be read as era-averaged, not time-invariant, quantities.

4.4 Future directions¶

  1. Structural validation. Map the convergent drivers onto HA head structure (e.g. AlphaFold / experimental structures) and test epitope-alteration vs. stability mechanisms.
  2. Break the linkage. Add strains that decouple the large H3N2 blocks, or incorporate deep-mutational-scanning escape maps as interventional data to move from observational discovery toward identified effects.
  3. Temporal / clade structure. The leave-one-era-out test (Section 3.9) already shows the driver identities are stable across decades while their magnitudes drift; a time-resolved causal graph or an explicitly non-stationary effect model (e.g. era-varying coefficients) is the natural next step to capture that drift rather than average over it.
  4. Cross-lineage transfer. Extend to the B/Yamagata and B/Victoria panels shipped in the repo and test whether the convergence framework generalizes.
  5. Interventional KAN. Use the learned causal parents to constrain the KAN's inputs, yielding a predictor whose importances are causal by construction.

Returning to the decision that opened this study — the twice-yearly bet on which strains next season's vaccine should target — the contribution here is not a more accurate predictor but a more honest one. A model that names which residues drive escape, that reports how confident it is in each, and that separates genuine effect from phylogenetic confounding is precisely the object surveillance needs in order to reason about strains it has never seen serologically. That the pipeline rediscovers the classical head epitopes from titers alone, with no structural input, is evidence the causal framing is tracking real biology rather than dataset idiosyncrasy. The honest limitations — a sink-star the data reject in detail, effect magnitudes that drift across decades, and large linkage blocks that no observational method can resolve — are not failures of the approach but a map of exactly where intervention, denser sampling, and structural follow-up must take over. Reading antigenic distance from sequence remains the goal; reading its causes from sequence is the step that would let surveillance anticipate escape rather than only measure it.

5. References¶

  1. Smith, D. J., Lapedes, A. S., de Jong, J. C., Bestebroer, T. M., Rimmelzwaan, G. F., Osterhaus, A. D. M. E., Fouchier, R. A. M. (2004). Mapping the antigenic and genetic evolution of influenza virus. Science 305(5682), 371–376.
  2. Bedford, T., Suchard, M. A., Lemey, P., Dudas, G., Gregory, V., Hay, A. J., McCauley, J. W., Russell, C. A., Smith, D. J., Rambaut, A. (2014). Integrating influenza antigenic dynamics with molecular evolution. eLife 3, e01914.
  3. Du, E., Zhong, Z., Wang, P., et al. (2023). DPCIPI: A pre-trained deep learning model for predicting cross-immunity between drifted strains of Influenza A/H3N2. arXiv:2302.00926.
  4. Grantham, R. (1974). Amino acid difference formula to help explain protein evolution. Science 185(4154), 862–864.
  5. Spirtes, P., Glymour, C., Scheines, R. (2000). Causation, Prediction, and Search (2nd ed.). MIT Press. (PC algorithm.)
  6. Chickering, D. M. (2002). Optimal structure identification with greedy search. Journal of Machine Learning Research 3, 507–554. (GES.)
  7. Zhang, J. (2008). On the completeness of orientation rules for causal discovery in the presence of latent confounders and selection bias. Artificial Intelligence 172(16–17), 1873–1896. (FCI.)
  8. Liu, Z., Wang, Y., Vaidya, S., et al. (2024). KAN: Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks. arXiv:2404.19756.
  9. Pearl, J. (2009). Causality: Models, Reasoning, and Inference (2nd ed.). Cambridge University Press. (Backdoor adjustment / do-calculus.)
  10. Chen, T., Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A scalable tree boosting system. KDD 2016, 785–794.

Data: influenza-hi-antigenic-distance repository (CC0). Code and this notebook are released alongside it. Causal discovery uses the causal-learn library; the KAN is a custom PyTorch implementation in src/bspline_kan.py. The full pipeline — linkage collapse, causal discovery, B-spline KAN, and cross-method convergence — is packaged as the reusable kan-causal-antigenic-workflow skill.
